title: Citations and Bibliography
text: PRIOGRID aggregates data many third-party providers. sources licensed Creative Commons (CC-) similar terms require attribution. Beyond legal compliance, citing data providers gives credit due helps readers trace provenance results. PRIOGRID makes easy: citation metadata embedded package linked variable.

title: Citations and Bibliography

PANGAEA, https://doi.org/10.1594/PANGAEA.921846}, #>   copyright = {info:eu-repo/semantics/openAccess}, #>   langid = {english}, #> } #>  #> @Article{harrisVersion4CRU2020, #>   title = {Version 4 of the {{CRU TS}} Monthly High-Resolution Gridded Multivariate Climate Dataset}, #>   author = {Ian Harris and Timothy J. Osborn and Phil Jones and David Lister}, #>   year = {2020}, #>   month = {apr}, #>   journal = {Scientific Data}, #>   volume = {7}, #>   number = {1}, #>   pages = {109}, #>   issn = {2052-4463}, #>   doi = {10.1038/s41597-020-0453-3}, #>   abstract = {CRU TS (Climatic Research Unit gridded Time Series) is a widely used climate dataset on a 0.5{$^\circ$} latitude by 0.5{$^\circ$} longitude grid over all land domains of the world except Antarctica. It is derived by the interpolation of monthly climate anomalies from extensive networks of weather station observations. Here we describe the construction of a major new version, CRU TS v4. It is updated to span 1901--2018 by the inclusion of additional station observations, and it will be updated annually. The interpolation process has been changed to use angular-distance weighting (ADW), and the production of secondary variables has been revised to better suit this approach. This implementation of ADW provides improved traceability between each gridded value and the input observations, and allows more informative diagnostics that dataset users can utilise to assess how dataset quality might vary geographically.}, #> }

title: Citations and Bibliography
text: chain variable citation : pgvariables maps variable names source UUIDs. pgsources maps source UUIDs BibTeX keys (citation_keys column). inst/REFERENCES.bib holds full bibliography (~200+ entries). can inspect manually:
code: pgvariables$name → pgvariables$source_ids → pgsources$citation_keys → REFERENCES.bib # Find the source IDs for a variable pgvariables[pgvariables$name == "cru_tmp", ] #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   name    static source_ids                           #>   <chr>   <lgl>  <chr>                                #> 1 cru_tmp FALSE  ac037134-3567-49d9-a3ba-64f37c1ee698  # Look up citation keys in pgsources source_id <- pgvariables[pgvariables$name == "cru_tmp", "source_ids"] pgsources[pgsources$id == source_id, c("source_name", "citation_keys")] #> # A tibble: 0 × 2 #> # ℹ 2 variables: source_name <chr>, citation_keys <chr>

title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
code: priogrid/ ├── R/ │   ├── data_{source}.R        # read_*() and gen_*() functions for each source │   ├── config.R               # pg_config(), pg_set_config(), pg_set_rawfolder() │   ├── build_priogrid.R       # calc_pg(), load_pgvariable(), read_pg_static(), etc. │   ├── utility.R              # robust_transformation(), rast_to_df(), prio_blank_grid() │   ├── references.R           # pgcitations(), get_bibliography() │   ├── source_class.R         # Source R6 class (dev-only: used in devtools::load_all()) │   └── download_data.R        # pgsearch(), pg_rawfiles(), check_pgsourcefiles() ├── data_raw/ │   ├── sources.csv            # Add new source here (54 entries) │   ├── variables.csv          # Add new variable here (32 entries) │   ├── pgsources.R            # Regenerates data/pgsources.rda from sources.csv │   └── pgvariables.R          # Regenerates data/pgvariables.rda from variables.csv ├── data/ │   ├── pgsources.rda          # Compiled metadata (auto-generated, do not edit) │   └── pgvariables.rda        # Compiled metadata (auto-generated, do not edit) ├── inst/ │   ├── REFERENCES.bib         # Full bibliography — add new BibTeX entries here │   └── extdata/urls/          # Multi-file URL lists (one .txt per source) └── tests/testthat/     └── test-build_priogrid.R  # Integration tests for gen_*() functions

title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: Every data source needs row data_raw/sources.csv. Required fields: Optional fields: aws_bucket, aws_region, prio_mirror, tags, reference_keys. Generate UUID id column using uuid::UUIDgenerate():
code: uuid::UUIDgenerate() # "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890"

title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: development mode (devtools::load_all()), use Source R6 class validate new source adding CSV: Source class validates required fields, checks spatial_extent temporal_resolution use allowed vocabulary, verifies citation keys exist inst/REFERENCES.bib.
code: new_source <- Source$new(   source_name          = "My New Dataset",   source_version       = "1.0",   license              = "CC BY 4.0",   website_url          = "https://example.com/data",   spatial_extent       = "World",   temporal_resolution  = "Yearly",   citation_keys        = "doeNewDataset2025",   download_url         = "https://example.com/data/dataset_v1.zip",   tags                 = "climate, land cover" ) new_source  # prints validation report; warns if citation key not in REFERENCES.bib

what: Step 2: Write read_*() and gen_*() Functions
title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: Create new file R/data_mynewsource.R.

previous_headings: Step 2: Write read_*() and gen_*() Functions
title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: read function downloads (via get_pgfile()) returns raw data sf SpatRaster object: get_pgfile() automatically downloads file present locally (automatic_download = TRUE config).
code: #' Read My New Dataset #' #' Downloads and imports My New Dataset. #' #' @return An \code{sf} object #' @export #' @references #' \insertRef{doeNewDataset2025}{priogrid} read_mynewsource <- function() {   f <- get_pgfile(     source_name    = "My New Dataset",     source_version = "1.0",     id             = "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890"   )   sf::st_read(f) }

previous_headings: Step 2: Write read_*() and gen_*() Functions
title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: generate function transforms raw data PRIOGRID grid returns SpatRaster. signature must accept config:
code: #' My New Variable #' #' Computes my_new_variable for each PRIOGRID cell. #' #' @param config A \code{pg_config} object. Defaults to \code{\link{pg_current_config}()}. #' @return A \code{SpatRaster} object #' @export #' @references #' \insertRef{doeNewDataset2025}{priogrid} gen_my_new_variable <- function(config = pg_current_config()) {   raw <- read_mynewsource()    # Reproject if needed   pg_crs <- sf::st_crs(config$crs)   if (sf::st_crs(raw) != pg_crs) {     raw <- sf::st_transform(raw, pg_crs)   }    # Convert to raster and re-grid to PRIOGRID resolution   raw_rast <- terra::rasterize(terra::vect(raw), prio_blank_grid(config),                                field = "my_value_column", fun = "mean")    r <- robust_transformation(raw_rast, agg_fun = "mean", config = config)    names(r) <- "my_new_variable"   return(r) }

previous_headings: Step 2: Write read_*() and gen_*() Functions
title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: robust_transformation() handles reprojection, cropping, aggregation, disaggregation, final resampling single call. standard way re-grid raster: Common agg_fun values: "mean", "sum", "max", "min", "modal" (categorical).
code: robust_transformation(   r             = raw_rast,      # Any SpatRaster (any resolution / CRS / extent)   agg_fun       = "mean",        # Aggregation function for higher-res inputs   disagg_method = "near",        # Disaggregation for lower-res inputs   config        = config         # Target config )

previous_headings: Step 2: Write read_*() and gen_*() Functions
title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: time-varying variables, iterate pg_dates(config) combine layers:
code: gen_my_yearly_variable <- function(config = pg_current_config()) {   dates <- pg_dates(config)   layers <- lapply(dates, function(d) {     raw <- read_mynewsource_for_year(lubridate::year(d))  # your reader     r   <- robust_transformation(raw, agg_fun = "mean", config = config)     names(r) <- as.character(d)     r   })   do.call(c, layers)  # stack into a multi-layer SpatRaster }

title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: Add row data_raw/variables.csv: name must match string passed names(r) gen_*() equal gen_{name} without prefix. static TRUE variables without time dimension, FALSE time-varying. source_ids UUID sources.csv. Multiple sources comma-separated. Regenerate pgvariables.rda: Verify:
code: name,static,source_ids my_new_variable,FALSE,a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 source("data_raw/pgvariables.R") pgvariables[pgvariables$name == "my_new_variable", ]

title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: PRIOGRID uses roxygen2 Markdown support Rdpack citation references. Key tags: - @param config — always document config parameter gen_*() functions - @return — describe function returns - @export — user-facing functions must exported - @references \insertRef{key}{priogrid} — links inst/REFERENCES.bib Regenerate documentation:
code: devtools::document()

title: Contributing to PRIOGRID
text: Add tests tests/testthat/. gen_* function, minimal test: Run tests:
code: # tests/testthat/test-data_mynewsource.R test_that("gen_my_new_variable returns a SpatRaster", {   skip_if_not_installed("terra")   cfg <- pg_config(     nrow = 36, ncol = 72,   # 5-degree resolution for fast tests     start_date = as.Date("2010-12-31"),     end_date   = as.Date("2010-12-31")   )   r <- gen_my_new_variable(config = cfg)   expect_s4_class(r, "SpatRaster")   expect_equal(names(r), "my_new_variable") }) devtools::test()

text: Test full pipeline:
code: cfg <- pg_config()  # 1. Calculate calc_pg("my_new_variable", config = cfg)  # 2. Load as raster r <- load_pgvariable("my_new_variable", config = cfg) terra::plot(r)  # 3. Load as table pg_tv <- read_pg_timevarying(config = cfg) "my_new_variable" %in% names(pg_tv)  # 4. Check citations resolve pgcitations("my_new_variable")

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
text: spatial temporal parameters held pg_config object. current session config accessed : Create custom config pg_config(): Date convention: Use last day temporal increment. yearly data December measurement dates, use 1990-12-31. monthly data January measurement dates, use 1990-01-31.
code: pg_current_config() #> PRIO-GRID config: #>   nrow: 360  #>   ncol: 720  #>   crs: epsg:4326  #>   extent: -180 180 -90 90  #>   temporal_resolution: 1 year  #>   start_date: 1850-12-31  #>   end_date: 2026-04-29  #>   verbose: TRUE  #>   automatic_download: TRUE cfg <- pg_config(   nrow = 180,   ncol = 360,   crs  = "epsg:4326",   extent = c(xmin = -180, xmax = 180, ymin = -90, ymax = 90),   temporal_resolution = "1 year",   start_date = as.Date("1990-12-31"),   end_date   = as.Date("2020-12-31") ) cfg #> PRIO-GRID config: #>   nrow: 180  #>   ncol: 360  #>   crs: epsg:4326  #>   extent: -180 180 -90 90  #>   temporal_resolution: 1 year  #>   start_date: 1990-12-31  #>   end_date: 2020-12-31  #>   verbose: TRUE  #>   automatic_download: TRUE

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
text: pg_set_config() modifies current session config place: fields specify changed. Reset defaults time:
code: pg_set_config(   temporal_resolution = "1 month",   start_date = as.Date("2000-01-31"),   end_date   = as.Date("2005-12-31") ) pg_current_config()$temporal_resolution #> [1] "1 month" pg_reset_config() pg_current_config()$temporal_resolution  # back to "1 year" #> [1] "1 year"

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
text: Custom builds stored disk using two 6-character MD5 hashes: Spatial hash — derived nrow, ncol, crs, extent Temporal hash — derived temporal_resolution, start_date, end_date hashes uniquely identify configuration without encoding full parameter set folder name. file path looks like: example, default config produces: change spatial parameter, spatial hash changes PRIOGRID treat new, independent build. prevents accidental mixing data incompatible configurations. Important: PRIOGRID cell IDs (pgid) depend spatial configuration. Data different configs joined pgid.
code: {rawfolder}/priogrid/custom/{pkg_version}/{spatial_hash}/{temporal_hash}/ # Internal helpers (not exported, shown for illustration) cfg_default <- pg_config() cat("Spatial hash:", priogrid:::get_spatial_hash(cfg_default), "\n") #> Spatial hash: 244f73 cat("Temporal hash:", priogrid:::get_temporal_hash(cfg_default), "\n") #> Temporal hash: 6ffd78

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
text: pgout_path() returns storage path current config: official release:
code: cfg <- pg_config(nrow = 180, ncol = 360) pgout_path(config = cfg) # {rawfolder}/priogrid/custom/3.0.1/a1b2c3/d4e5f6/ pgout_path(version = "3.0.1", type = "05deg_yearly") # {rawfolder}/priogrid/releases/3.0.1/05deg_yearly/

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
text: calc_pg() calculates variables saves disk. calls gen_*() function variable: default, existing files skipped. Use overwrite = TRUE recalculate: calculation, load results load_pgvariable():
code: cfg <- pg_config(   nrow = 180,   ncol = 360,   start_date = as.Date("2000-12-31"),   end_date   = as.Date("2020-12-31") )  # Calculate a single variable calc_pg("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean", config = cfg)  # Calculate multiple variables calc_pg(c("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean", "naturalearth_cover"), config = cfg)  # Calculate all variables (takes a long time) calc_pg(config = cfg) calc_pg("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean", overwrite = TRUE, config = cfg) r <- load_pgvariable("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean", config = cfg) terra::plot(r)

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
code: cfg_europe <- pg_config(   nrow   = 100,   ncol   = 140,   extent = c(xmin = -25, xmax = 45, ymin = 34, ymax = 72),   temporal_resolution = "1 month",   start_date = as.Date("2010-01-31"),   end_date   = as.Date("2020-12-31") )  calc_pg("cru_tmp", config = cfg_europe)  r <- load_pgvariable("cru_tmp", config = cfg_europe) terra::plot(r[["2015-07-31"]], main = "Mean temperature, July 2015 (Europe)")

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
code: # Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area, centred on Asia cfg_laea <- pg_config(   nrow = 200,   ncol = 200,   crs  = "+proj=laea +lon_0=90 +lat_0=45 +datum=WGS84 +units=m" )  calc_pg("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean", config = cfg_laea) r <- load_pgvariable("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean", config = cfg_laea) terra::plot(r)

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
text: pg_list_custom() shows custom builds currently stored disk:
code: customs <- pg_list_custom() # [1] a1b2c3/d4e5f6: nrow=180, ncol=360, 2000-12-31 to 2020-12-31, 2 vars, created 2025-01-15 # [2] e7f8g9/h0i1j2: nrow=100, ncol=140, 2010-01-31 to 2020-12-31, 1 vars, created 2025-01-16  # Load data from a listed config read_pg_static(config = customs[[1]])

title: Custom Spatial and Temporal Configurations
text: Load custom builds tabular form way official release:
code: cfg <- pg_config(nrow = 180, ncol = 360)  pg_static_custom <- read_pg_static(config = cfg) pg_tv_custom     <- read_pg_timevarying(config = cfg)

title: Getting Started with PRIOGRID
text: PRIOGRID stores raw downloaded data processed outputs single folder machine. path persists across R sessions. set, can retrieve time:
code: library(priogrid)  # Set the data folder (run once; persists across sessions) pg_set_rawfolder("/path/to/your/data/folder") pg_rawfolder()

title: Getting Started with PRIOGRID
text: PRIOGRID session-scoped configuration object controls spatial temporal parameters grid. defaults match official PRIOGRID release: can inspect individual fields: configuration effect current R session. See Custom Configurations vignette change .
code: pg_current_config() #> PRIO-GRID config: #>   nrow: 360  #>   ncol: 720  #>   crs: epsg:4326  #>   extent: -180 180 -90 90  #>   temporal_resolution: 1 year  #>   start_date: 1850-12-31  #>   end_date: 2026-04-29  #>   verbose: TRUE  #>   automatic_download: TRUE cfg <- pg_current_config() cfg$nrow #> [1] 360 cfg$temporal_resolution #> [1] "1 year"

title: Getting Started with PRIOGRID
text: Download current official PRIOGRID release single call. downloads zip archive extracts raw data folder: see releases available: download happens — subsequent calls skipped unless set overwrite = TRUE.
code: download_priogrid() download_priogrid(list_releases = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with PRIOGRID
text: downloaded, load full dataset data.table. requires spatial libraries.

title: Getting Started with PRIOGRID
text: Static variables change time (e.g., terrain elevation, country border distances): row PRIOGRID cell identified pgid. Columns variable names.
code: pg_static <- read_pg_static() head(pg_static)

title: Getting Started with PRIOGRID
text: Time-varying variables measurement_date column addition pgid: tables can joined together pgid, merged data.
code: pg_tv <- read_pg_timevarying() head(pg_tv)

title: Getting Started with PRIOGRID
code: library(data.table)  # Subset to a specific year pg_2020 <- pg_tv[measurement_date == as.Date("2020-12-31")]  # Join static and time-varying on pgid pg_merged <- merge(pg_static, pg_2020, by = "pgid")

title: Understanding PRIOGRID Metadata
text: three metadata objects form chain: ’s full lookup single variable: retrieve full bibliography:
code: pgvariables$source_ids → pgsources$id → pgsources$citation_keys → REFERENCES.bib # 1. Find source IDs for a variable var_row <- pgvariables[pgvariables$name == "cru_tmp", ] source_ids <- strsplit(var_row$source_ids, ", ")[[1]] source_ids #> [1] "ac037134-3567-49d9-a3ba-64f37c1ee698"  # 2. Look up source metadata src <- pgsources[pgsources$id %in% source_ids, c("source_name", "source_version", "citation_keys", "license")] src #>        source_name source_version         citation_keys #> 45 CRU Climate tmp          v4.09 harrisVersion4CRU2020 #>                                                                       license #> 45 https://www.nationalarchives.gov.uk/doc/open-government-licence/version/3/  # 3. Get citation keys bibkeys <- unlist(strsplit(src$citation_keys, "; ")) bibkeys #> [1] "harrisVersion4CRU2020" get_bibliography(bibkeys[1]) #> [1] I. Harris, T. J. Osborn, P. Jones, et al. "Version 4 of the CRU TS #> Monthly High-Resolution Gridded Multivariate Climate Dataset". In: #> _Scientific Data_ 7.1 (Apr. 2020), p. 109. ISSN: 2052-4463. DOI: #> 10.1038/s41597-020-0453-3.

title: Understanding PRIOGRID Metadata
text: pgsearch() searches across text fields pgsources using regex: returns named list matches search field: Search temporal resolution: Search spatial extent:
code: results <- pgsearch("population") # Sources matching by name results$in_name[, c("source_name", "source_version")] #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #>   source_name              source_version #>   <chr>                    <chr>          #> 1 GHSL GHS Population Grid R2023  # Sources matching by tag results$in_tags[, c("source_name", "tags")] #> # A tibble: 9 × 2 #>   source_name                                tags                                #>   <chr>                                      <chr>                               #> 1 ETH ICR EPR Core                           ethnicity, population, social stru… #> 2 ETH ICR GeoEPR                             ethnicity, population, social stru… #> 3 MCC-PIK DOSE – Subnational Economic Output population, socio - economic        #> 4 GHSL GHS-BUILT-C                           demographic, infrastructure, land … #> 5 GHSL GHS-BUILT-H                           demographic, land use, population   #> 6 GHSL GHS Population Grid                   demographic, infrastructure, land … #> 7 GHSL GHS-BUILT-S                           demographic, land use, population   #> 8 GHSL GHS Settlement Model Grid             demographic, population, urbanizat… #> 9 GHS-WUP-DEGURBA                            urban extent, population pgsearch("Monthly")$in_temporal_resolution[, c("source_name", "temporal_resolution")] #> # A tibble: 9 × 2 #>   source_name                                  temporal_resolution #>   <chr>                                        <chr>               #> 1 UCDP GED                                     Higher than monthly #> 2 Armed Conflict Location & Event Data (ACLED) Higher than monthly #> 3 Geocoded Peacekeeping Operations (Geo-PKO)   Monthly             #> 4 ETH ICR cShapes                              Higher than monthly #> 5 CRU Climate tmp                              Monthly             #> 6 CRU Climate pre                              Monthly             #> 7 CRU Climate pet                              Monthly             #> 8 Global SPEI database                         Monthly             #> 9 UCDP GED                                     Higher than monthly pgsearch("World")$in_spatial_extent[, c("source_name", "spatial_extent")] #> # A tibble: 46 × 2 #>    source_name                                           spatial_extent #>    <chr>                                                 <chr>          #>  1 EOG Annual VIIRS Nighttime Lights                     World          #>  2 Global Area Equipped for Irrigation Dataset 1900-2015 World          #>  3 GlobalDataLab Area Database                           World          #>  4 SEDAC Global Subnational Infant Mortality Rates       World          #>  5 Li Nighttime                                          World          #>  6 World Bank Global Subnational Poverty Atlas (GSAP)    World          #>  7 ETH ICR EPR Core                                      World          #>  8 UCDP GED                                              World          #>  9 ETH ICR GeoEPR                                        World          #> 10 WIDE Education Inequalities                           World          #> # title: Understanding PRIOGRID Metadata
text: pg_rawfiles() returns data frame files PRIOGRID can download, URLs expected local paths: Filter specific source: Download specific source: Get local path downloaded file:
code: files <- pg_rawfiles() head(files[, c("source_name", "source_version", "filename")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   source_name                                        source_version filename     #>   <chr>                                              <chr>          <chr>        #> 1 SEDAC Food Insecurity Hotspots                     v1             SEDAC Food … #> 2 SEDAC Global Subnational Infant Mortality Rates    v2.01          SEDAC Globa… #> 3 Li Nighttime                                       v8             Li Nighttim… #> 4 World Bank Global Subnational Poverty Atlas (GSAP) Oct. 2024      World Bank … #> 5 ETH ICR EPR Core                                   2023           ETH ICR EPR… #> 6 UCDP GED                                           24.1           UCDP GED/24… files[files$source_name == "ETH ICR cShapes", c("source_name", "filename", "url")] #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   source_name     filename                                                 url   #>   <chr>           <chr>                                                    <chr> #> 1 ETH ICR cShapes ETH ICR cShapes/2.0/ec3eea2e-6bec-40d5-a09c-e9c6ff2f8b6… http… ucdp_files <- pg_rawfiles() |> dplyr::filter(source_name == "UCDP GED") download_pg_rawdata(file_info = ucdp_files) get_pgfile(   source_name    = "ETH ICR cShapes",   source_version = "2.0",   id             = "ec3eea2e-6bec-40d5-a09c-e9c6ff2f8b6b" )

title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: prio_blank_grid() creates empty SpatRaster filled PRIOGRID cell IDs (pgid). useful understand grid geometry attach values plotting:
code: pg <- prio_blank_grid() pg # class       : SpatRaster # dimensions  : 360, 720, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) # resolution  : 0.5, 0.5  (x, y) # extent      : -180, 180, -90, 90  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) # coord. ref. : lon/lat WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) # source(s)   : memory # name        : pgid  terra::plot(pg)

title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: load_pgvariable() downloads (needed) returns PRIOGRID variable SpatRaster. default uses official release: layer corresponds one time step. Layer names dates "YYYY-MM-DD" format: Select single layer name:
code: ucdp <- load_pgvariable("ucdp_ged") ucdp names(ucdp)[1:5] # [1] "1989-12-31" "1990-12-31" "1991-12-31" "1992-12-31" "1993-12-31" ucdp_2020 <- ucdp[["2020-12-31"]] terra::plot(log1p(ucdp_2020), main = "UCDP GED events (log scale), 2020")

title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: Static variables (time dimension) returned named list single-layer SpatRaster objects:
code: static_rasters <- read_pg_static(as_raster = TRUE) names(static_rasters)  # Plot terrain ruggedness terra::plot(static_rasters[["ruggedterrain_elevation_mean"]])

title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: Time-varying variables returned named list, element multi-layer SpatRaster:
code: tv_rasters <- read_pg_timevarying(as_raster = TRUE)  # Each element is a SpatRaster with one layer per year tv_rasters[["cru_tmp"]]

title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: pgsearch() searches meta-data across source names, versions, tags, spatial extent, temporal resolution: Search tag find related variables:
code: results <- pgsearch("climate") # Returns a named list of data frames for each search category  # Which sources match by tag? results$in_tags[, c("source_name", "tags")] #> # A tibble: 4 × 2 #>   source_name          tags                                    #>   <chr>                <chr>                                   #> 1 CRU Climate tmp      climate, validation, temperature        #> 2 CRU Climate pre      climate, validation, precipitation      #> 3 CRU Climate pet      climate, validation, evapotranspiration #> 4 Global SPEI database drought index, climate conflict_sources <- pgsearch("conflict")$in_tags conflict_sources[, c("source_name", "temporal_resolution")] #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   source_name                                  temporal_resolution #>   <chr>                                        <chr>               #> 1 UCDP GED                                     Higher than monthly #> 2 Armed Conflict Location & Event Data (ACLED) Higher than monthly #> 3 UCDP GED                                     Higher than monthly

what: Converting Rasters Back to Data Frames
title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: rast_to_df() converts SpatRaster data.table pgid (measurement_date time-varying data): time-varying data, set static = FALSE:
code: r <- load_pgvariable("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean") df <- rast_to_df(r, static = TRUE, varname = "ruggedterrain_elevation_mean") head(df) #    pgid  ruggedterrain_elevation_mean # 1:    1                           NA # 2:    2                           NA # ... r <- load_pgvariable("cru_tmp") df <- rast_to_df(r, static = FALSE, varname = "cru_tmp") head(df) #    pgid measurement_date  cru_tmp # 1:    1       1901-12-31       NA

title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: terra supports stacking, arithmetic, many operations. patterns:
code: # Stack two variables for combined analysis elev <- load_pgvariable("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean") pop  <- load_pgvariable("ghsl_population_grid")[["2020-12-31"]]  # Crop to a region (e.g., Sub-Saharan Africa) africa_ext <- terra::ext(c(-20, 55, -35, 40)) elev_africa <- terra::crop(elev, africa_ext) pop_africa  <- terra::crop(pop, africa_ext)  terra::plot(c(elev_africa, log1p(pop_africa)),             main = c("Elevation", "Population (log)"))

title: Accessing PRIOGRID as Rasters
text: save_pgvariable() saves SpatRaster back PRIOGRID data folder wrapped .rds file:
code: r <- gen_ruggedterrain_elevation_mean() save_pgvariable(r, "ruggedterrain_elevation_mean")  # Later, load it back: r2 <- load_pgvariable("ruggedterrain_elevation_mean")

title: Calculate Grid Cell Coverage by International State System Boundaries (cShapes 2.0) — cshapes_cover_share
text: function uses CShapes 2.0 historical boundary data calculate proportion PRIO-GRID cell covered countries part international state system measurement date. function: Filters CShapes data boundaries valid measurement date Performs exact area-weighted extraction using exactextractr package Returns proportional coverage values (0-1) grid cell Masks non-land areas final result coverage values represent fraction PRIO-GRID cell area falls within internationally recognized state boundaries.

text: excluded character vector specifying EPR status codes classify "excluded". Valid EPR status codes include: "DISCRIMINATED": Groups facing systematic discrimination "POWERLESS": Groups lacking political influence "SELF-EXCLUSION": Groups voluntarily excluding politics "MONOPOLY": Groups holding exclusive power "DOMINANT": Groups dominant power positions "SENIOR PARTNER": Senior coalition partners "JUNIOR PARTNER": Junior coalition partners "IRRELEVANT": Groups without political relevance "STATE COLLAPSE": Groups state collapse periods Default c("DISCRIMINATED", "POWERLESS", "SELF-EXCLUSION").

ISSN 0738-8942, 1549-9219. doi:10.1177/0738894210393217 . 2024-10-21.  Vogt M, Bormann N, Rüegger S, Cederman L, Hunziker P, Girardin K (2015). “Integrating Data Ethnicity, Geography, Conflict: Ethnic Power Relations Data Set Family.” Journal Conflict Resolution, 59(7), 1327–42. 2024-10-21.

title: Generate Multi-Temporal Regionally Excluded Ethnic Groups Coverage — gen_geoepr_reg_excluded
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate excluded groups coverage with default exclusion categories excluded_coverage <- gen_geoepr_reg_excluded()  # Examine the structure print(excluded_coverage)  # View available time periods time_periods <- names(excluded_coverage) head(time_periods)  # Plot coverage for specific period terra::plot(excluded_coverage[[1]],             main = "Excluded Ethnic Groups Coverage")  # Use custom exclusion definition (include only discriminated groups) discriminated_only <- gen_geoepr_reg_excluded(excluded = "DISCRIMINATED")  # Compare different exclusion definitions powerless_only <- gen_geoepr_reg_excluded(excluded = "POWERLESS") combined_exclusion <- gen_geoepr_reg_excluded(excluded = c("DISCRIMINATED", "POWERLESS"))  # Analyze temporal changes in exclusion first_period <- excluded_coverage[[1]] last_period <- excluded_coverage[[nlyr(excluded_coverage)]] exclusion_change <- last_period - first_period terra::plot(exclusion_change, main = "Change in Excluded Group Presence")  # Calculate total excluded area over time total_excluded_cells <- terra::global(excluded_coverage > 0, "sum", na.rm = TRUE) plot(total_excluded_cells$sum, type = "l",      main = "Number of Cells with Excluded Groups Over Time") } # }

title: Generate Multi-Temporal Peacekeeping Operations Count Data — gen_geopko_operations_count
text: Cil D, Fjelde H, Hultman L, Nilsson D (2020). “Mapping Blue Helmets: Introducing Geocoded Peacekeeping Operations (Geo-PKO) Dataset.” Journal Peace Research, 57(2), 360–370. ISSN 0022-3433, 1460-3578. doi:10.1177/0022343319871978 . 2024-10-21.

title: Generate Multi-Temporal Peacekeeping Operations Count Data — gen_geopko_operations_count
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate peacekeeping operations count data pko_counts <- gen_geopko_operations_count()  # Examine the structure print(pko_counts)  # View available time periods time_periods <- names(pko_counts) head(time_periods) tail(time_periods)  # Plot operations count for specific period terra::plot(pko_counts[[1]],             main = paste("Peacekeeping Operations Count:", time_periods[1]))  # Find periods with highest peacekeeping activity total_operations <- terra::global(pko_counts, "sum", na.rm = TRUE) max_activity_period <- which.max(total_operations$sum) terra::plot(pko_counts[[max_activity_period]],             main = paste("Peak Activity Period:", time_periods[max_activity_period]))  # Analyze temporal trends in peacekeeping deployment yearly_totals <- terra::global(pko_counts, "sum", na.rm = TRUE) plot(1:nlyr(pko_counts), yearly_totals$sum,      type = "l", main = "Total Peacekeeping Operations Over Time",      xlab = "Time Period", ylab = "Total Operations Count")  # Identify hotspots of peacekeeping activity total_pko_activity <- terra::app(pko_counts, sum, na.rm = TRUE) terra::plot(total_pko_activity,             main = "Cumulative Peacekeeping Operations")  # Compare early vs. recent periods early_period <- pko_counts[[1:5]]  # First 5 periods recent_period <- pko_counts[[(nlyr(pko_counts)-4):nlyr(pko_counts)]]  # Last 5 periods early_total <- terra::app(early_period, sum, na.rm = TRUE) recent_total <- terra::app(recent_period, sum, na.rm = TRUE) terra::plot(c(early_total, recent_total),             main = c("Early Period PKO", "Recent Period PKO")) } # }

title: Generate Multi-Temporal Peacekeeping Troop Deployment Data — gen_geopko_troops_count
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate peacekeeping troop deployment data pko_troops <- gen_geopko_troops_count()  # Examine the structure print(pko_troops)  # View available time periods time_periods <- names(pko_troops) head(time_periods)  # Plot troop deployment for specific period terra::plot(pko_troops[[1]],             main = paste("Peacekeeping Troops:", time_periods[1]))  # Find periods with largest troop deployments total_troops <- terra::global(pko_troops, "sum", na.rm = TRUE) max_deployment_period <- which.max(total_troops$sum) terra::plot(pko_troops[[max_deployment_period]],             main = paste("Peak Deployment:", time_periods[max_deployment_period]))  # Analyze temporal trends in troop deployment yearly_troop_totals <- terra::global(pko_troops, "sum", na.rm = TRUE) plot(1:nlyr(pko_troops), yearly_troop_totals$sum,      type = "l", main = "Total Peacekeeping Troops Over Time",      xlab = "Time Period", ylab = "Total Troops Deployed")  # Identify major deployment locations cumulative_troops <- terra::app(pko_troops, sum, na.rm = TRUE) terra::plot(cumulative_troops,             main = "Cumulative Peacekeeping Troop Deployments")  # Compare troop numbers vs. operation counts pko_operations <- gen_geopko_operations_count() # Compare specific periods period_idx <- 10  # Example period terra::plot(c(pko_operations[[period_idx]], pko_troops[[period_idx]]),             main = c("Operations Count", "Troop Numbers"))  # Analyze troop concentration (troops per operation) troops_per_op <- pko_troops / pko_operations terra::plot(troops_per_op[[period_idx]],             main = "Average Troops per Operation") } # }

text: Schiavina M, Melchiorri M, Pesaresi M, Jacobs-Crisioni C, Dijkstra L (2025). “GHS-WUP-DEGURBA R2025A – GHS-WUP DEGURBA Settlement Layers, Application Degree Urbanisation Methodology (Stage ) GHS-WUP-POP R2025A, Multitemporal (1975-2100).” doi:10.2905/1c049178-ab00-4bbc-b638-3e3c19daaacb . title: Generate GHS-WUP-DEGURBA urban proportion grid — gen_ghs_wup_degurba_urban
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate PRIO-GRID level urbanization rasters urban_pg <- gen_ghs_wup_degurba_urban()  # Inspect structure print(urban_pg)  # Plot urbanization for 2020 terra::plot(urban_pg[["2020-12-31"]],             main = "PRIO-GRID Urban Proportion 2020")  # Compute urbanization change over time urban_1990 <- urban_pg[["1990-12-31"]] urban_2020 <- urban_pg[["2020-12-31"]] change <- urban_2020 - urban_1990 terra::plot(change, main = "Urbanization Change 1990–2020")  # Identify highly urbanized cells (>75% urban) highly_urban <- urban_pg[["2020-12-31"]] > 0.75 terra::plot(highly_urban, main = "Highly Urbanized Areas")  # Calculate global urban area trends urban_stats <- terra::global(urban_pg, "mean", na.rm = TRUE) years <- as.numeric(substr(names(urban_pg), 1, 4)) plot(years, urban_stats$mean,      type = "l", main = "Global Urbanization Trends",      xlab = "Year", ylab = "Mean Urban Proportion") } # }

title: Generate UCDP GED Battle-Related Deaths Variable — gen_ucdp_ged
text: SpatRaster object multiple layers, one time interval defined pg_date_intervals. layer contains: Cell values: Sum best fatality estimates events cell period Layer names: Character representation interval end dates NA values: Grid cells recorded events outside state system

title: Generate UCDP GED Battle-Related Deaths Variable — gen_ucdp_ged
text: function generates standard UCDP GED variable PRIO-GRID using: "best" fatality estimate UCDP GED three types organized violence (state-based, non-state, one-sided) Temporal distribution fatalities across PRIO-GRID intervals Spatial filtering exclude events without sub-national precision Events filtered include spatial precision codes 1-5, represent locations identified sub-national level better. Events coded national level (where_prec = 6) higher uncertainty (where_prec = 7) excluded. filtering approach may result undercounting, particularly regions precise event locations difficult determine. function uses "best" fatality estimate, represents UCDP's assessment likely number fatalities based available sources. Alternative estimates (low, high) can accessed ucdp_ged.

title: Generate UCDP GED Battle-Related Deaths Variable — gen_ucdp_ged
text: Processing time depends size UCDP GED dataset exclusion imprecisely located events known limitation custom configurations, use ucdp_ged directly

title: Generate UCDP GED Battle-Related Deaths Variable — gen_ucdp_ged
text: Sundberg R, Melander E (2013). “Introducing UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset.” Journal Peace Research, 50(4), 523–532. ISSN 0022-3433, 1460-3578. doi:10.1177/0022343313484347 . 2024-10-21. Davies S, Pettersson T, Sollenberg M, Öberg M (2025). “Organized Violence 1989–2024, Challenges Identifying Civilian Victims.” Journal Peace Research, 62(4), 1223–1240. ISSN 0022-3433. doi:10.1177/00223433251345636 . 2025-06-11.

title: Generate UCDP GED Battle-Related Deaths Variable — gen_ucdp_ged
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate standard UCDP GED variable ged_fatalities <- ged_ucdp_ged()  # Examine the result print(ged_fatalities)  # Check number of time periods terra::nlyr(ged_fatalities)  # Plot first time period terra::plot(ged_fatalities[[1]],             main = paste("Fatalities:", names(ged_fatalities)[1]))  # Calculate annual totals annual_total <- terra::app(ged_fatalities, sum, na.rm = TRUE) terra::plot(annual_total, main = "Total Fatalities Across All Periods",             col = hcl.colors(100, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE))  # Identify hotspots hotspots <- annual_total > 100 terra::plot(hotspots, main = "Conflict Hotspots (>100 fatalities)") } # }

title: Apply urban classification to GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data — ghs_wup_degurba

title: Apply urban classification to GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data — ghs_wup_degurba
text: DEGURBA classification codes represent: 30: Urban centres (cities) 23: Dense urban cluster 22: Semi-dense urban cluster 21: Suburban peri-urban 13: Rural cluster 12: Low density rural 11: low density rural 10: Water bodies uninhabited areas function performs following operations: Reads high-resolution GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data Reclassifies cells 1 (urban) match urban_definition, 0 otherwise Aggregates PRIO-GRID resolution using mean (proportion urban area) Applies nearest-neighbor resampling exact PRIO-GRID alignment slight nearest neighbor resampling applied get exact PRIO-GRID extent.

Options include "best", "low", "high", "event_count" counting events rather fatalities.

title: Rasterize UCDP GED Events for a Single Time Interval — rasterize_ged_crossection
text: SpatRaster object aggregated values specified time interval. Layer name set interval end date.

title: Rasterize UCDP GED Events for a Single Time Interval — rasterize_ged_crossection
text: function performs following operations: Transforms event coordinates match PRIO-GRID projection Filters events overlapping specified time interval Removes events spatial precision code >= 6 (sub-national precision required) Filters specified violence types Rasterizes events summing specified fatality variable counting events Spatial precision filtering removes events coded national level higher uncertainty, may lead undercounting regions.

title: Reads the EPR Core 2023 data — read_epr
text: tibble object

title: Reads the EPR Core 2023 data — read_epr
text: EPR Core tracks political status ethnic groups politically relevant country-year. function: Downloads EPR Core dataset ETH ICR data repository Converts temporal variables (, ) proper Date objects (gwsdate, gwedate) Adds date_interval utility column temporal operations Returns data tibble format analysis dataset categorizes ethnic groups based access executive power, ranging monopoly dominance discrimination exclusion. Groups considered politically relevant either represented government, engage political competition, face systematic discrimination. Political status categories typically include: Monopoly: Group holds exclusive power Dominance: Group holds dominant power position Senior/Junior Partner: Group shares power coalition Powerless: Group lacks political influence Discriminated: Group faces systematic discrimination Self-exclusion: Group voluntarily excludes politics

title: Reads the EPR Core 2023 data — read_epr
text: Vogt M, Bormann N, Rüegger S, Cederman L, Hunziker P, Girardin K (2015). “Integrating Data Ethnicity, Geography, Conflict: Ethnic Power Relations Data Set Family.” Journal Conflict Resolution, 59(7), 1327–42. 2024-10-21.

title: Reads the EPR Core 2023 data — read_epr
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read EPR Core data epr_data <- read_epr()  # Examine the structure print(epr_data) str(epr_data)  # Check available columns names(epr_data)  # View temporal coverage range(epr_data$gwsdate, na.rm = TRUE) range(epr_data$gwedate, na.rm = TRUE)  # Examine unique countries unique_countries <- unique(epr_data$country) head(sort(unique_countries), 10)  # Filter to specific country usa_groups <- epr_data[epr_data$gwid == 2, ]  # USA print(usa_groups)  # Analyze power status distribution if("status" %in% names(epr_data)) {   table(epr_data$status) }  # Filter groups active in specific year target_date <- as.Date("2010-01-01") active_2010 <- epr_data[target_date %within% epr_data$date_interval, ] nrow(active_2010)  # Analyze temporal changes for specific group # example_group <- epr_data[epr_data$group == "African Americans", ] # View(example_group) } # }

title: Reads the Geocoded Peacekeeping Operations (Geo-PKO) data — read_geopko
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read Geo-PKO data geopko_data <- read_geopko()  # Examine the structure print(geopko_data) str(geopko_data)  # Check available columns names(geopko_data)  # View temporal coverage range(geopko_data$mydate, na.rm = TRUE)  # Examine unique missions if("mission" %in% names(geopko_data)) {   unique_missions <- unique(geopko_data$mission)   head(sort(unique_missions), 10) }  # Plot peacekeeping deployment locations plot(sf::st_geometry(geopko_data),      main = "UN Peacekeeping Deployment Locations",      pch = 16, cex = 0.5)  # Filter to specific time period recent_deployments <- geopko_data[geopko_data$mydate >= as.Date("2010-01-01"), ] plot(sf::st_geometry(recent_deployments),      main = "Recent Peacekeeping Deployments (2010+)",      pch = 16, cex = 0.5)  # Analyze deployment intensity by year geopko_data$year_deployed <- lubridate::year(geopko_data$mydate) yearly_deployments <- table(geopko_data$year_deployed) plot(names(yearly_deployments), yearly_deployments,      type = "l", main = "Peacekeeping Deployments Over Time",      xlab = "Year", ylab = "Number of Deployments")  # Filter to specific mission (example) # monusco_data <- geopko_data[grepl("MONUSCO", geopko_data$mission), ] # plot(sf::st_geometry(monusco_data), main = "MONUSCO Deployments") } # }

title: Reads the GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data — read_ghs_wup_degurba
text: GHS-WUP-DEGURBA provides urbanization classification 1 km spatial resolution multiple time periods globally. function: Downloads zipped GHS-WUP-DEGURBA raster files data repository Extracts TIF files zip archives (cached avoid repeated extraction) Loads combines multiple raster layers single multi-temporal raster Standardizes layer names using PRIO-GRID date format consistency Provides urbanization classification data 5-year intervals (typically 1975-2030) DEGURBA classification uses following codes: 30: Urban centres (cities) 23: Dense urban cluster 22: Semi-dense urban cluster 21: Suburban peri-urban 13: Rural cluster 12: Low density rural 11: low density rural 10: Water bodies uninhabited areas

title: Reads the GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data — read_ghs_wup_degurba

European Commission, Statistical Office European Union (2021). “Applying Degree Urbanisation — Methodological Manual Define Cities, Towns Rural Areas International Comparisons.” doi:10.2785/706535 . title: Reads the GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data — read_ghs_wup_degurba
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data # Warning: This involves large file downloads and processing degurba <- read_ghs_wup_degurba()  # Examine the structure print(degurba)  # View available time periods time_periods <- names(degurba) print(time_periods)  # Plot urbanization classification for specific year terra::plot(degurba[["2020-12-31"]],             main = "Global Urbanization Classification 2020")  # Compare urbanization change over time urban_1990 <- degurba[["1990-12-31"]] urban_2020 <- degurba[["2020-12-31"]] terra::plot(urban_2020 - urban_1990,             main = "Urbanization Change 1990-2020")  # Extract urbanization for specific region (example: crop to extent) # asia_extent <- terra::ext(60, 140, -10, 50) # asia_urban <- terra::crop(degurba, asia_extent) # terra::plot(asia_urban[[nlyr(asia_urban)]], main = "Asia Urbanization")  # Calculate frequency of each urbanization class freq_2020 <- terra::freq(degurba[["2020-12-31"]]) print(freq_2020) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Read GHSL population grid data # Warning: This involves large file downloads and processing ghsl_population <- read_ghsl_population_grid()  # Examine the structure print(ghsl_population)  # View available time periods time_periods <- names(ghsl_population) print(time_periods)  # Plot population for specific year terra::plot(ghsl_population[["2020-12-31"]],             main = "Global Population Distribution 2020")  # Compare population change over time pop_1990 <- ghsl_population[["1990-12-31"]] pop_2020 <- ghsl_population[["2020-12-31"]] pop_change <- pop_2020 - pop_1990 terra::plot(pop_change, main = "Population Change 1990-2020")  # Extract population for specific region (example: crop to extent) # europe_extent <- terra::ext(-10, 40, 35, 70) # europe_pop <- terra::crop(ghsl_population, europe_extent) # terra::plot(europe_pop[[nlyr(europe_pop)]], main = "Europe Population")  # Calculate total global population by year global_pop_totals <- terra::global(ghsl_population, "sum", na.rm = TRUE) years <- as.numeric(substr(names(ghsl_population), 1, 4)) plot(years, global_pop_totals$sum / 1e9,      type = "l", main = "Global Population Trends",      xlab = "Year", ylab = "Population (Billions)") } # }

title: Process UCDP GED Data with Temporal Fatality Distribution — ucdp_ged
text: function implements sophisticated fatality distribution algorithm : Calculates temporal overlap event PRIO-GRID intervals Distributes fatalities proportionally based number overlapping days Uses integer allocation remainder distribution preserve total counts Handles edge cases events span multiple periods single fatalities Validates distributed fatalities sum original event totals distribution algorithm ensures : Total fatalities preserved (- -counting) Single-fatality events assigned exactly one period Fractional allocations resolved using largest remainder method Events without sub-national spatial precision (where_prec >= 6) excluded final rasterization, may result undercounting regions.

what: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
title: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
text: Identifies delineates contiguous urban extent surrounding specified geographic location using GHS-WUP-DEGURBA (Degree Urbanisation) data. function extracts urban patch contains input coordinates, providing polygon boundary connected urban area.

title: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
code: urban_extent(   lon,   lat,   measurement_date,   urban_definition = c(21, 22, 23, 30),   max_extent = 1e+06 )

title: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
text: lon Numeric. Longitude location interest (WGS84, decimal degrees). lat Numeric. Latitude location interest (WGS84, decimal degrees). measurement_date Character. Date extract urban extent, format "YYYY-MM-DD" (e.g., "2020-12-31"). Must match one available dates GHS-WUP-DEGURBA dataset (typically 5-year intervals: 1975, 1980, ..., 2030). urban_definition Numeric vector DEGURBA codes classify urban. Default c(21, 22, 23, 30) (suburban, semi-dense, dense, urban centres). Valid codes : 10, 11, 12, 13, 21, 22, 23, 30. See Details code meanings. max_extent Numeric. Maximum search radius meters around input coordinates. Default 1000000 (1000 km). limits area searched connected urban patches improves computational efficiency.

title: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
text: sf polygon object representing contiguous urban extent containing input location. polygon returned coordinate reference system specified current config CRS. input location classified urban, function may return empty polygon fail.

title: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
text: function performs following workflow: Creates circular buffer radius max_extent around input coordinates Extracts GHS-WUP-DEGURBA data specified measurement_date Crops raster buffer area computational efficiency Reclassifies cells binary urban (1) non-urban (0) based urban_definition Identifies contiguous urban patches using 8-directional connectivity Determines patch contains input coordinates Extracts dissolves contiguous urban patch single polygon DEGURBA classification codes represent: 30: Urban centres (cities) 23: Dense urban cluster 22: Semi-dense urban cluster 21: Suburban peri-urban 13: Rural cluster 12: Low density rural 11: low density rural 10: Water bodies uninhabited areas Contiguity: Urban patches defined using 8-directional connectivity, meaning cells considered connected share edge corner.

title: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
text: function requires input coordinates fall within urban area Larger max_extent values increase computational time memory usage urban definition significantly affects resulting extent Stricter definitions (e.g., code 30) produce smaller extents Broader definitions (e.g., codes 21-30) produce larger extents Processing time varies size urban area

title: Extract contiguous urban extent around a location — urban_extent
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Extract urban extent for Oslo, Norway (2020) oslo_extent <- urban_extent(   lon = 10.763063,   lat = 59.935320,   measurement_date = "2020-12-31" )  # Plot the result plot(sf::st_geometry(oslo_extent), main = "Oslo Urban Extent 2020")  # Extract urban extent for New Delhi, India (2020) delhi_extent <- urban_extent(   lon = 77.231487,   lat = 28.612738,   measurement_date = "2020-12-31" )  # Compare urban extent over time for the same location paris_1975 <- urban_extent(   lon = 2.3522,   lat = 48.8566,   measurement_date = "1975-12-31" ) paris_2020 <- urban_extent(   lon = 2.3522,   lat = 48.8566,   measurement_date = "2020-12-31" )  # Calculate urban area expansion area_1975 <- sf::st_area(paris_1975) area_2020 <- sf::st_area(paris_2020) expansion <- (area_2020 - area_1975) / area_1975 * 100 cat("Urban expansion:", round(expansion, 1), "%\n")  # Use stricter urban definition (only urban centres) tokyo_core <- urban_extent(   lon = 139.6917,   lat = 35.6895,   measurement_date = "2020-12-31",   urban_definition = 30 )  # Use broader urban definition (including all urbanized areas) tokyo_metro <- urban_extent(   lon = 139.6917,   lat = 35.6895,   measurement_date = "2020-12-31",   urban_definition = c(21, 22, 23, 30) )  # Reduce search radius for smaller cities kongsvinger <- urban_extent(   lon = 12.000669,   lat = 60.190700,   measurement_date = "2020-12-31",   max_extent = 50e3  # 50 km radius )  # Extract multiple cities and compare cities <- data.frame(   name = c("London", "Paris", "Berlin"),   lon = c(-0.1276, 2.3522, 13.4050),   lat = c(51.5074, 48.8566, 52.5200) )  city_extents <- lapply(1:nrow(cities), function(i) {   urban_extent(     lon = cities$lon[i],     lat = cities$lat[i],     measurement_date = "2020-12-31"   ) })  # Calculate areas city_areas <- sapply(city_extents, sf::st_area) cities$area_km2 <- as.numeric(city_areas) / 1e6 print(cities) } # }
